preamble: The transformation from the diphthong /ei/ to /oi/ represents a notable sound change in the history of certain languages, particularly evident in the development of Old French. This shift involves a modification of the initial vowel sound within the diphthong, altering its place and manner of articulation.
phoneticEffects: This phonetic shift primarily involves the backing and rounding of the initial vowel element of the diphthong

examples.note: From Latin 'me(n)sem' (month), where Old French /eː/ diphthongized to /ei/ before shifting to /oi/ in most dialects.
From Latin 'Pictavum', referring to the region of Poitou.

sources: Ringe, D. (2006). A Linguistic History of English: From Proto-Indo-European to Proto-Germanic. Oxford University Press.
Pope, M. K. (1952). From Latin to Modern French with Especial Consideration of Anglo-Norman Phonology and Morphology. Manchester University Press.
Kibler, W. W. (1984). An Introduction to Old French. Modern Language Association of America.
Selections from Early Middle English, 1130-1250. (n.d.). Project Gutenberg.
Restoring a Vernacular Jewish Voice: The Old French Elegy of Troyes. (n.d.). OSRJL Tutorials.
Historical Linguistics: Toward a Twenty-First Century Reintegration. (n.d.). DOKUMEN.PUB.
